perturbation.original_text: Any Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives orally or visually, shall be identified as such prior to disclosure and summarized in a writing provided by the Disclosing Party to t he Receiving Party within thirty (30) days of the date of initial disclosure. In 2/5 case of disagreement regarding said summary, the Receiving Party shall make any objections in writing within thirty (30) days of receipt of the written summary.
perturbation.changed_text: Any Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives orally or visually, shall be identified as such prior to disclosure and summarized in a writing provided by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party within ninety (90) days of the date of initial disclosure. In case of disagreement regarding said summary, the Receiving Party shall make any objections in writing within fifteen (15) days of receipt of the written summary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the objection period for the summary of orally disclosed confidential information from 30 days to 15 days could be problematic under legal standards that mandate reasonable timeframes. While Italian law provides flexibility, reducing the objection timeframe significantly could be deemed insufficient, potentially leading to disputes over the fairness of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Art. 1341 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Art. 1341 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 1341 of the Italian Civil Code addresses general conditions of contract and requires that onerous clauses are specifically approved in writing to be valid. While the 15 day timeframe alone may not violate 1341, it can increase the likelihood of the clause being deemed overly onerous if the receiving party has less time to review and object the summaries, potentially making the clause invalid if not explicitly approved.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the objection period from 30 to 15 days, which the Law Explanation says could be deemed overly onerous under Art. 1341 of the Italian Civil Code, particularly if the receiving party has less time to review summaries. While the snippet from the Italian Civil Code doesn't explicitly state a specific timeframe, it references Art. 1341, which deals with onerous clauses. The change increases the likelihood that the clause could be invalidated if not explicitly approved. This creates a contradiction as it undermines the receiving party's ability to adequately review and object, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving party must, at the end of this Agreement or within seven days following written instruction by the Disclosing Party to do so, return or destroy all Confidential Information in the Receiving Party 's possession, custody or control.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving party must, at the end of this Agreement or within one day following written instruction by the Disclosing Party to do so, return or destroy all Confidential Information in the Receiving Party 's possession, custody or control.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe to return confidential information to just 1 day following written instruction may be considered unreasonable, especially if significant volumes of data are involved. Italian law requires 'good faith' (Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code) in contract execution, and such a short timeframe might be seen as a breach of that principle.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code mandates that contracts be performed in good faith. Requiring return/destruction of confidential information within one day of notice may not provide sufficient time for the receiving party to comply, violating the good faith principle and potentially creating legal challenges regarding the enforceability of this clause.

perturbation.justification: The change from seven days to one day for returning or destroying confidential information creates an inconsistency with the principle of 'good faith' as stipulated by Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code (as mentioned in the Perturbation and Law Explanations). The scraped snippet 1 contains "art. 1375", referencing the location of the law itself. One day might be deemed an unreasonably short timeframe to allow for proper compliance, potentially violating Article 1375.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding this, any Party may terminate its involvement with this Agreement by giving 30 (thirty) days written notice (registered letter with proof of receipt) to the other Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding this, any Party may terminate its involvement with this Agreement by giving 7 (seven) days written notice (registered letter with proof of receipt) to the other Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the written notice period for termination to 7 days might be seen as insufficient, especially considering the reliance parties place on agreements and the disruptions that might result from abrupt termination. Italian law emphasizes fair dealing, and the reduced notice could be considered unfair in some contexts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the termination notice to seven days could be deemed a violation of good faith principles under Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code. The abrupt termination may create hardship for other parties involved, especially if they relied on the agreement for strategic planning or partnerships.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 30 days written notice for contract termination, whereas the changed text reduces this to 7 days. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because it involves a time-sensitive obligation. While the scraped law snippets are missing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that reducing the notice period could violate the principle of good faith under Italian law (Article 1375 of the Italian Civil Code) because such a sudden change may create hardship for other involved parties.

perturbation.original_text: Termination of this Agreement shall not affect any obligation imposed by this Agreement with 4/5 respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to termination , the obligations of non -use and non-disclosure of Confidential Information shall survive for 3 (three) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Termination of this Agreement shall not affect any obligation imposed by this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to termination, the obligations of non-use and non-disclosure of Confidential Information shall survive for 6 (six) months from the date of termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival period for confidentiality obligations to 6 months might be insufficient to protect the Disclosing Party's confidential information, depending on the industry and nature of information. While not directly illegal, it undermines the purpose of the NDA, which may not be upheld in court.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1322 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1322 of the Italian Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Italian Civil Code's Article 1322 ensures contractual autonomy but must be reasonable and not against mandatory rules. Setting a short, 6 month confidentiality period after termination, especially given the nature of confidential research and business information exchanged, could be seen as unfairly limiting the disclosing party's rights and possibly violating the 'social utility' of contracts by undermining the trust necessary for collaboration, making it difficult for the contract to achieve its intended purpose.

perturbation.justification: The change from a 3-year survival period for confidentiality obligations to 6 months creates a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'. The law explanation states that this change could be seen as unfairly limiting the disclosing party's rights and undermining the trust necessary for collaboration. Although the snippet provides Art. 1322, about contractual autonomy, but indicates that this autonomy must be reasonable and not against mandatory rules. Reducing the survival period may be considered unreasonable given the nature of the information being protected.
